sections.markdown:
# DEFPACKAGE Basics

A **package** is a namespace of symbols. `defpackage` creates one (or updates one) and declares its relationships to other packages.

```lisp
(defpackage #:my-app
  (:use #:common-lisp #:alexandria)
  (:export #:start #:stop)
  (:import-from #:cl-ppcre #:scan #:register-groups-bind)
  (:shadow #:count))
```

The options:

* `:use` — packages whose **exported** symbols are made directly visible inside this package. Almost every package uses `#:common-lisp` (a.k.a. `#:cl`) so you can write `defun` instead of `cl:defun`.
* `:export` — symbols this package promises as its public API. Other packages can refer to them as `my-app:start`.
* `:import-from` — pull specific symbols from another package directly into this one (without `:use`'ing all of it).
* `:shadow` — declare that *our* symbol of this name takes precedence over any same-named symbol that would otherwise be inherited via `:use`. Without this, the import collision is an error.

Name designators can be strings (`"MY-APP"`), symbols (`'my-app`), or — by convention — *uninterned* symbols (`#:my-app`). The uninterned form is preferred so that loading the file doesn't pollute the current package with a stray `MY-APP` symbol.

sections.explain: The package now exists. Its name is the upper-cased string DEMO.
Omitting :USE gives a package that inherits NOTHING — not even DEFUN. Rarely what you want, but legal.

sections.prompt: Define a package named "MY-MATH" that uses CL and exports a symbol SQUARE. Then evaluate (length (package-shadowing-symbols (find-package "MY-MATH"))) — your test will check this returns 0 (no shadowing).
